evidence: All HTTP endpoints have no authentication or authorization — this is the consistent design for a local corpus research tool; no route has an auth decorator and no route is missing one relative to its siblings

Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * is set on every response via HttpApiUtils.sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, and sendOptionsResponse — CORS policy is uniformly open and consistently applied

Input validation is inconsistent across sibling endpoints: handleSemanticFieldExplore (line 468-473) and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (line 606-610) wrap numeric param parsing in try/catch(NumberFormatException); handleSemanticField (lines 708-709), handleSemanticFieldExamples (line 786), and handleConcordanceExamples (line 813) do not

The /api/bcql POST endpoint passes the user-supplied BCQL query string directly to CorpusQueryLanguageParser.parse() without length limits or sanitization — intentional for a local tool but inconsistent with the required-param validation in /api/sketch/{lemma} and /api/concordance/examples

Word1/word2 presence is validated in handleConcordanceExamples (returns 400 if missing), but the seed parameter in handleSemanticFieldExplore only returns an empty result if blank rather than a 400 error — inconsistent error signaling for missing required input
No admin/privileged routes exist — there is no asymmetry between protected and unprotected routes to exploit

The server binds to 0.0.0.0 implicitly via InetSocketAddress(port) — it listens on all interfaces, not just localhost, which means the no-auth design is wider in scope than a localhost-only binding would imply

strengths: There are no partial-auth situations — no route has auth while its siblings do not; the no-auth design is applied uniformly
CORS headers are set via shared HttpApiUtils helpers on every response type (JSON, binary, error, options), ensuring the open-CORS policy is consistently applied without per-handler drift
The tool has no user accounts, roles, or permissions — no permission strings exist as magic literals or constants because none are needed
authorization_consistency.issue_character: Authorization consistency is structurally sound (no auth by design), but the trust boundary is inconsistently applied: some endpoints validate required inputs and return 400, others silently accept missing/invalid params, and the server binds to all interfaces rather than localhost despite carrying no auth.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The no-auth design is intentional and consistent for a local research tool, so the authorization dimension is largely N/A in the classic sense. The score is held below 80 by two genuine trust-boundary inconsistencies: numeric parameter validation is applied in some handlers and omitted in siblings (allowing NumberFormatException at runtime), and missing required inputs are signaled differently (400 vs. empty result) across sibling handlers. A tool explicitly documented as localhost-only would also benefit from binding to 127.0.0.1 rather than 0.0.0.0.

issues.summary: Missing required input parameters produce inconsistent responses across sibling endpoints: some return 400, others return an empty/partial result
evidence: handleConcordanceExamples: if (word1 == null || word1.isEmpty() || word2 == null || word2.isEmpty()) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Missing required parameters"); return; } — returns 400
handleSemanticFieldExplore: if (seed == null || seed.isBlank()) returns ExplorationResult.empty(seed) — the handler returns 200 with an empty result instead of 400
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: if seeds.length < 2 returns 400 — consistent with concordance examples but not with single-seed explore
A caller sending a blank seed to /api/semantic-field/explore receives a 200 with empty results and no indication that the input was invalid
issues.suggestion: Standardize: return 400 with a descriptive error message whenever a required parameter is missing or blank, across all endpoints. Update handleSemanticFieldExplore to check seed.isBlank() and return 400 before calling the explorer.

issues.summary: The HTTP server binds to all network interfaces (0.0.0.0) rather than localhost, making the unauthenticated API reachable from other hosts on the network
evidence: server = com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer.create(new InetSocketAddress(port), 0) — InetSocketAddress(port) binds to the wildcard address (0.0.0.0), not InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port)
The API has no authentication and passes BCQL queries directly to the index parser — reachable from any host on the LAN with no guard
CLAUDE.md and README.md describe the server as a local tool; the binding does not match the intended trust boundary
issues.suggestion: Change to: new InetSocketAddress(InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress(), port) or new InetSocketAddress("127.0.0.1", port) to restrict to localhost. If external access is needed, document it explicitly and note the no-auth implication.

root_causes: The tool was designed for local use but was not hardened to actually enforce that boundary at the network layer
Endpoint validation was added incrementally and the convention (400 vs empty-result for missing input) was never normalized across all handlers
